title: RBA looks to get ahead of the curve on jobs
full_text:
The Reserve Bank has been doing some deep thinking about one of the oldest questions in economics: how much does unemployment need to rise before inflation comes down.
The answer, according to deputy governor Andrew Hauser, depends on where you start.
In a wide-ranging lecture to the Economics Society in Melbourne on Tuesday, Hauser revisits the work of New Zealand-born economist Bill Phillips, whose 1958 paper first mapped the relationship between unemployment and wages.
The key insight – one that was largely forgotten for decades – is that the relationship is curved.
When unemployment is very low, even a small further tightening of the labour market can send wages and prices sharply higher. When unemployment is higher, it has far less effect.
For much of the period before Covid-19, central banks around the world – including, to a degree, the RBA – treated this relationship as roughly flat. Small changes in unemployment were assumed to produce only small changes in inflation, regardless of conditions.
During the so-called Great Moderation of the 1990s and 2000s, things seemed stable enough that a straight line looked like a reasonable shortcut.
“Persistently low and stable inflation in the pre-Covid-19 period led many policymakers to conclude that the Phillips curve they were facing was not only linear but also relatively flat,” Hauser says.
The consequences of that assumption proved painful. When inflation surged across the world after Covid-19 – against a backdrop of low unemployment and easy money – it came as “a nasty surprise”, he says. Yet it was, he argues, “exactly what a nonlinear Phillips curve would predict”.
The RBA’s forecasting models did include a curved rather than a straight-line relationship, based on work by former RBA economists Guy Debelle and James Vickery. But even those models underestimated the post-Covid-19 inflation surge.
Part of the problem was that the models were built on data from quieter times – periods when the economy was mostly on the flatter part of the curve.
New RBA research is now trying to better capture why the curve bends the way it does. Three drivers stand out.
First, wages are sticky on the way down. Workers resist pay cuts even in a weak economy, which means lower unemployment has an outsized upward effect on wages when the labour market is already tight.
Second, job vacancies don’t rise smoothly as unemployment falls. When unemployment is already low, it becomes increasingly hard for firms to find workers, so they must offer sharply higher wages to attract them. This nonlinear relationship between vacancies and unemployment, known as the Beveridge curve, helps explain both why inflation looked contained before Covid-19 and why it exploded afterwards.
Third, when inflation is already running hot, firms change their prices more frequently. New RBA research shows this shift in pricing behaviour accounted for between half a percentage point and 1.25 percentage points of Australia’s post-Covid-19 inflation rise. Once firms are already adjusting prices regularly, any further rise in their costs – from demand or supply – gets passed on quickly.
All of this, Hauser says, directly shaped the RBA’s decision to raise rates at its February, March and May meetings.
The February rise reflected concern that “unexpectedly rapid increases in demand growth, coupled with anaemic growth in the economy’s supply potential, reduced spare capacity and increased inflationary pressures”.
By March, the outbreak of conflict in the Middle East added a potential cost shock on top of already elevated capacity pressures. By May, those risks were crystallising.
“In general, the more non-linear the Phillips curve is, the stronger is the case for central banks to take proactive policy action to reduce excessive capacity pressures,” Hauser says.
There is a silver lining, because being on the steep part of the Phillips Curve cuts both ways.
Inflation can rise sharply with small increases in demand, but “timely policy steps to reduce inflationary pressures, of the kind we have taken, should also have a proportionally smaller unemployment cost on the way down,” Hauser says.
“That beneficial effect should be further amplified if pre-emptive policy also helps anchor inflation expectations, preventing larger shifts upwards in the curve.” The RBA’s May forecasts projected inflation returning to target with only a limited increase in joblessness – consistent with that logic.
Hauser is quick to add a word of caution. The same labour market forces that make inflation accelerate when unemployment falls can also make it hard to bring unemployment back down once it rises.
“Unemployment goes up by the elevator but down by the stairs,” he says – a dynamic vividly illustrated by the early 1990s recession, with long-lasting effects on young workers in particular.
He also stresses that policymakers rarely know in real time exactly where they sit on the curve, or what is driving it. But on the current outlook, Hauser keeps his cards close.
Lower oil prices – reflecting prospects of a Middle East resolution – would help ease inflation. “But a full resolution is not yet assured, and we still have work to do to reduce inflation in Australia, which remains far too high,” he says.
